patch.value:
## Context
Every pm read command today enumerates .agents/pm/<type>/*.toon (O(workspace)); history is one JSONL stream per item; locks are per-item files; output size is unbounded by default. This is fine at 1.6k items and breaks down at 100k+.

## Decision drivers
- Context building must stay sub-second and bounded-token at 1M items (philosophy: project management = context management).
- Thousands of agents mutate concurrently: index maintenance must be as safe as appendLineAtomic; no global write hotspots.
- Git-native storage is a hard constraint: everything must remain diffable, mergeable, and recoverable from the working tree alone (index must be derivable/rebuildable, never authoritative).
- Zero data-format breakage: fan-out/sharding ships behind format_version gating with a migration.

## Options to evaluate (decision pending)
1. Read index: single compact ledger file vs per-type index vs embedded (SQLite/LanceDB-adjacent) cache under .agents/pm/runtime (gitignored, rebuildable).
2. Directory fan-out: hash-bucket subdirs per type vs date-bucketed vs flat-with-index-only.
3. Pagination: cursor tokens (stable sort key) vs offset; default --limit for list/search/context at scale.
4. Claim/lock scaling: per-item locks already shard naturally; singleton files (settings/session) need the pm-bgcu fix plus contention budget.
5. Git growth: gc/history-compact cadence, git gc/packing guidance, optional archive tier for closed items.
ADR: workspace scale-out strategy — indexed reads, storage fan-out, and bounded-output contracts for 100k-1M-item workspaces (proposed)

Proposed ADR (open=proposed, close=accepted per pm-m2u1 convention) that fixes the ground rules for the scale-out initiative epic. Duplicate check 2026-07-06: searched scalability/sharding/index/pagination — no existing open or closed item covers workspace-scale architecture.

ADR records the decision for: (1) persistent read-index format + invalidation, (2) item-directory fan-out scheme + format_version migration path, (3) bounded-output/cursor-pagination contract, (4) claim/lock scaling approach, (5) git-repo growth policy (packing, history compaction cadence); status accepted (item closed) before dependent implementation tasks start
Fix the architectural rules that let pm stay sub-second and bounded-token as workspaces grow to 100k-1M items with thousands of concurrent agents
Without a scale strategy decided up front, index/sharding/pagination work would be ad-hoc and could require repeated storage migrations

Product goal explicitly targets workspaces of hundreds of thousands to millions of items with thousands of parallel agents; current read paths scan the full item directory and output is unbounded by default

value.author: maintainer-agent
value.text: Decision-point-3 input (2026-07-10 planning pass): pm-dfg0 now carries a grounded S1-S4 scoping note with the verified current pagination surface (list --limit/--offset unbounded-by-default; search --limit only; context --depth full = MAX_SAFE_INTEGER hole; no cursor anywhere). Recommendation from that analysis: cursor tokens over offset (opaque stable-sort-tuple cursor, id tiebreak) — offset re-scans degrade at 100k+ and break under concurrent mutation; a threshold-gated default limit preserves small-workspace ergonomics (pm-z436). The S1 cursor primitive can be spiked in the pm-rjqr SDK query facade as ADR input before this ADR formally lands.

value.author: maintainer-agent
value.text:
Decision inputs from the 2026-07-11 perf brainstorm (ledger on pm-9rxu):
- Option 1 (read index) gains a maintenance-strategy sub-decision: incremental index updates riding the existing mutation path (same hook family as background semantic refresh — write-time O(1) upkeep) vs rebuild-on-stale-read. Invalidation source candidate: mtime/stat journal, same keying the shipped three-tier metadata cache (ADR pm-vnie) already trusts.
- Any embedded store (SQLite/LanceDB-adjacent) stays a gitignored, rebuildable CACHE — never authoritative (ADR pm-i7i4). Rebuild cost at 1M items is itself a budgeted number (mi2x tier reports).
- Startup is near-floor (bundle + lazy imports + compile cache + lazy activation all shipped), so this ADR's read-path options carry the remaining order-of-magnitude; git-subprocess count on read paths is a fifth decision driver worth stating explicitly (pure reads may need zero git spawns).

author: maintainer-agent
message: Start scale-out architecture decision using measured 10k/100k local benchmark evidence; GitHub-hosted performance jobs explicitly excluded

value.author: maintainer-agent
value.text: Duplicate and evidence check: all-status searches for persistent metadata index and cursor pagination confirm pm-vnie is the closed three-tier stat-cache predecessor, pm-801d is the canonical derived-index implementation, and pm-dfg0 is the canonical bounded-output implementation. Local 100k evidence: CLI list 28.1s, search 32.0s, next 31.6s/192k estimated tokens; SDK reads 3.0-8.0s and next 126k tokens. Decision work is active; no GitHub-hosted performance workload will be introduced.

patch.value:
## Context

The existing three-tier metadata cache (pm-vnie) avoids reparsing unchanged item files, but every cold CLI process still enumerates and stats every item. The local scale harness (pm-mi2x) measures this at 100,000 items: CLI list/search/next take about 28-32 seconds; warm SDK reads take 3-8 seconds; next emits 126k-192k estimated tokens. The Git-native working tree remains authoritative per pm-i7i4.

## Decision

### 1. Derived read index and invalidation

Extend the existing runtime metadata-cache family into a versioned derived read index under `.agents/pm/runtime/`; do not introduce a database, server, or daemon. The index stores light metadata projections, stable ordering tuples, dependency edges, claim/actionability fields, source paths/formats, a context fingerprint, and per-type-directory generation signatures. Bodies and heavy collections remain separate opt-in tiers.

Normal reads may take an O(result) fast path when the index version, context fingerprint, item-directory signatures, and mutation generation agree. All pm writes use atomic replacement, so creates, deletes, moves, migrations, and mutations change a directory signature and invalidate the fast path. A stale, absent, corrupt, version-mismatched, or extension-hook-incompatible index transparently falls back to the canonical source scan and rebuilds the index. `pm reindex`, strict validation, repair, migration, and explicit cache-bypass paths always retain a source-scan mode. Direct edits to `.agents/pm` are outside the supported mutation contract; users must use pm mutations or `pm reindex` after such recovery edits.

Index maintenance must not create a singleton write bottleneck. Writes append bounded, atomic delta records or invalidate a generation marker while holding only the existing per-item lock; a compaction/rebuild folds deltas into immutable index snapshots. A crash can lose derived deltas, never source data: signature/generation mismatch forces rebuild.

### 2. Item-directory fan-out and format migration

Flat type directories remain readable. The scale layout uses deterministic two-level hash buckets derived from the normalized item id under each type folder. The storage `format_version` gates writes; migrations are lossless, resumable, lock-aware, and dual-read old/new layouts until completion. History and per-item locks use the same id-derived fan-out independently. No layout migration is implicit during an ordinary read.

### 3. Bounded output and cursor pagination

Use opaque, versioned base64url cursors over a stable ordering tuple plus id tiebreaker and a fingerprint of the normalized query/filter/sort contract. Cursor mismatch is an actionable error; offset remains a compatibility alias but is not the scale contract. Result envelopes expose `has_more`, `next_cursor`, applied limit, and total only when it is available without an extra full scan.

Small workspaces preserve current defaults. At the documented scale threshold, list/search/context/next apply a bounded default chosen to keep the normal agent payload below 5,000 estimated tokens, with an explicit truncation marker and continuation hint. Explicit `--limit`, `--all`, or full-depth compatibility controls remain available so no capability is removed.

### 4. Claim and lock scaling

Per-item locks remain the atomic test-and-set boundary and naturally shard by id. Claim-next walks bounded cursor pages and retries after a race loss. Singleton settings/session operations use their existing dedicated locks with bounded jitter; neither index reads nor index delta writes acquire one global workspace lock.

### 5. Git and history growth

Derived runtime indexes, benchmark fixtures, and reports generated outside committed docs stay gitignored. Canonical item/history files remain reviewable. `pm history-compact --all-over` is the supported checkpoint/prune mechanism; scale guidance will recommend an event/byte threshold and advisory `git gc`/maintenance cadence, but pm will not run destructive Git maintenance automatically. Archival of terminal items requires a separate format-versioned, reversible feature and is not part of read-index correctness.

## Consequences

- CLI, SDK, MCP, and packages share one SDK-first query/index contract.
- Correctness never depends on a derived store, and source-scan equivalence tests remain mandatory.
- Successful hot reads perform no Git subprocess and no O(workspace) item stat walk.
- Writes stay O(1) with per-item contention; occasional rebuild cost is measured locally at 10k/100k/1M.
- Cursor tokens are stable for an unchanged query generation and intentionally rejected after incompatible query/sort changes.
- GitHub Actions do not run scale benchmarks; performance evidence and regression checks run locally to avoid hosted-runner consumption.

## Evidence and implementation lineage

- pm-mi2x: deterministic local scale generator, measurements, reports, and budgets.
- pm-801d: derived index substrate and read-path adoption.
- pm-dfg0: cursor and bounded-output adoption.
- pm-0qg5: format-versioned item/history/lock fan-out.
- pm-o79o: history and Git growth automation/guidance.
- pm-lvyd / pm-2muu / pm-114v: claim throughput and lock semantics.

Accepted: versioned rebuildable derived index, hash-bucket migration contract, stable opaque cursors with scale-threshold bounded defaults, per-item lock scaling, and advisory Git/history growth policy
Scale implementation has an SDK-first, Git-native architecture contract with explicit correctness and fallback rules
Decision grounded in local 10k/100k benchmark evidence and linked to canonical implementation items; hosted GitHub performance workloads excluded

value.author: maintainer-agent
value.text: Decision evidence: recorded all five required decisions with implementation lineage and explicit local-only performance policy. The derived index is never authoritative, has source-scan fallback/equivalence, and avoids a singleton write lock; cursors preserve explicit full-output compatibility and small-workspace defaults.

patch.value: Accepted scale-out ADR: rebuildable SDK-first read index, format-versioned fan-out, cursor/bounded-output contract, sharded claim locking, and Git/history growth policy; grounded in local 100k benchmark evidence.
